straight_beat.name: Don Pellegrino
straight_beat.column:
The Mets’ bullpen has become a graveyard for hope. For weeks now, it’s been the same script: starters pitch well, the offense stalls, and the relievers—supposedly the team’s strength—fold like paper in a hurricane. Yesterday’s 1-2 loss to the Dodgers wasn’t just another game; it was a mirror held up to a roster that’s been built on the promise of resilience, only to reveal a foundation of cracks.  

The starters, for all their struggles, have been decent

optimist.name: Sheila 'Sheesh' Fortunato
optimist.column:
The Mets lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the sauce. You know what they say about pizza—sometimes the best slices come after the first bite. Same with baseball. We took a hit, sure, but there’s a whole loaf of bread in the oven, and I’m not done kneading it yet.  

Let’s start with the bullpen. Yeah, they blew the save, but that’s not the end of the story. It’s the middle of the script. The guys in the pen are still out there, still throwing, still fighting. You can’t win every game, but you can learn from every loss. And believe me, I’ve learned a lot from my own oven—like how a little smoke in the dough can make it taste better. Maybe that’s what

pessimist.name: Sal Marusiak
pessimist.column: The Mets lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your television out the window. Yesterday’s 2-1 defeat to the Dodgers wasn’t just another loss—it was a reminder of why we’ve all been waiting for the team to finally collapse in a dramatic, self-inflicted manner. The record now reads 7-11, 4.0 games behind in the NL East, and if you’re keeping score, that’s a .391 winning percentage. For a team
